exemplar.title: To what extent are the synthetic food colorants Allura Red (CI 16035), Ponceau 4R (CI 16255), and Sunset Yellow FCF (CI 15985) present in tomato sauce samples from street food vendors in Lakarsantri, Surabaya analyzed through Paper Chromatography?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent are the synthetic food colorants Allura Red (CI 16035), Ponceau 4R (CI 16255), and Sunset Yellow FCF (CI 15985) present in tomato sauce samples from street food vendors in Lakarsantri, Surabaya analyzed through Paper Chromatography?

strengths: Clear and purposeful communication of the investigationâs topic, with an explicit rationale linking background context to the research question.
Well-focused and consistently referenced research question, with appropriate acknowledgment of qualitative limitations.
Comprehensive description of methodology, including variable control and error discussion, demonstrating thoughtful experimental design.
weaknesses: Minor verbosity and occasional digressions in the introduction reduce concision.
Some methodological justifications (e.g. solvent choice, sampling representativeness) are only partly developed.
The phrasing âto what extentâ remains slightly ambiguous, limiting methodological precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments placed two elements solidly in the 5â6 band and one at the top of the 3â4 band. The investigation is communicated accurately and the question is well-focused, with a largely complete methodology. Minor issues of scope wording and justification prevent a perfect 6, but the overall coherence and purposefulness justify a score of 5.

strengths: Consistent and mostly precise use of chemical terminology (e.g. chromophore, retention factor).
Sources are appropriately selected and integrated to justify both background and interpretation of results.
Subject knowledge is coherent and demonstrates effective understanding of chromatography and dye chemistry.
weaknesses: Occasional inconsistency in term usage (e.g. LLE vs liquidâliquid extraction) and minor awkward formula renderings.
Some descriptive passages could be more analytical, particularly when applying regulatory data.
Mechanistic links (e.g. polarity vs Rf) are explained but not quantitatively connected to student data.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands were judged in the upper 3â4 band, with evidence of coherent subject knowledge, proper terminology, and relevant source use. While there were occasional lapses into description, the overall clarity and appropriateness merit placement in the 5â6 band, awarding a score of 5.

strengths: Research design is good: primary data from real-world samples and authenticated standards directly address the research question.
Analysis is clear and well-supported by Rf comparisons, with thoughtful discussion of precision and error sources.
Discussion demonstrates critical evaluation of limitations and links findings to health regulations and methodological improvements.
weaknesses: Quantitative depth is limited by absence of statistical tests or error propagation analyses.
Evaluation of source validity and broader chemical principles could be more comprehensive.
Some conclusions overreach the data (e.g. suggesting trace presence without quantitative evidence).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work aligns with the 7â9 band descriptors: research is good and relevant, analysis is clearly presented with supported conclusions, and discussion shows partially critical evaluation. Upgrading to a score of 7 accurately reflects these strengths while acknowledging areas needing deeper quantitative rigor.

strengths: Follows all required Extended Essay structural conventions (title page, word count, section headings, page numbering).
Figures, tables and bibliography are consistently formatted and properly referenced in text.
Logical organization enhances navigation and overall readability.
weaknesses: Occasional overly large tables disrupt flow and could be split or moved to appendices.
A few editing oversights (e.g. incomplete sentences, duplicate equations) slightly detract from polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and layout fully meet the top band criteria, with only minor lapses in flow and editorial precision. These small issues do not undermine the clear presentation, warranting the maximum score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay follows the **required EE structure**: title page with word count, abstract omitted (in line with current EE guidelines), table of contents, sequential sections (Introduction, Background, Methodology, Results, Discussion, Evaluation, Conclusion, References). Headings and sub-headings are logical and facilitate navigation. Minor issuesâoccasional long tables disrupting flow and some redundancyâslightly impair readability, but overall the structure is clearly appropriate, fitting the top of the 3â4 band (score 4).

The title and opening paragraphs present a **clear, specific and engaging description of the investigation**âidentifying the three dyes, the food matrix (tomato sauce from street vendors in Lakarsantri) and the analytical technique (paper chromatography). The introduction and background section supply sufficient contextual information on foodâdye use, regulation and health concerns, establishing an explicit rationale and purpose. Minor issues of verbosity and some digressions into general food-industry commentary reduce absolute precision, but the focus on detecting particular dyes remains evident throughout. Overall, communication is accurate and purposeful, fulfilling the descriptors of the 5â6 band, though not quite exemplary enough in concision and synthesis to merit the full 6.

Data are processed into averages, ranges and simple bar charts; the student compares Rf values logically to infer dye identity and comments on precision through range analysis. **Analysis is clear but remains largely descriptive and limited to direct Rf comparison**; no statistical tests, error propagation or deeper exploration of extraction efficiency are attempted. Conclusions about âpresenceâ or âabsenceâ occasionally over-interpret the data (e.g., suggesting trace dyes without quantitative evidence). Thus, analysis is **partly but not fully developed and only moderately supported**, fitting the lower half of the 4â6 band (score 4).

The student collects primary data from **four real-world samples and three authenticated standards**, and records multiple trials, indicating a reasonable breadth and relevance to the RQ. The extraction and chromatographic methods chosen are suitable for detecting the targeted dyes, and the comparison with standards directly addresses the research question. Nevertheless, the small sample size and qualitative nature of âpresence/absenceâ limit the investigationâs rigour. Overall, the research is **good and generally relevant**, aligning with the descriptors of the 4â6 band; a mid-high score of 5 is appropriate.

Throughout the essay the student demonstrates **sound knowledge of chromatography, dye chemistry and regulatory toxicology**. Key concepts such as polarity, chromophores, retention factor and liquidâliquid extraction are explained with reasonable accuracy. Chemical structures and functional-group discussions show understanding, yet some explanations remain textbook-like and do not always connect directly to the studentâs own data (e.g., polarity discussion not quantitatively linked to Rf trends). Minor inaccuracies in wording and a lack of depth in mechanistic explanation hold the work within the competent 3â4 band, meriting a score of 4.

The report employs **appropriate chemical terminology**âchromophore, azo dyes, retention factor, acceptable daily intake, etc.âwith general accuracy and consistency. Calculations are properly annotated and variables clearly defined. However, some terms (e.g., âLLEâ, âadsorbentâ vs âsorbentâ) are used inconsistently, and a few formulae/polynomial expressions are rendered awkwardly. Conceptual links (e.g., between polarity and migration distance) could be articulated more rigorously. These limitations prevent reaching the highest band, but the overall use of terminology is mostly accurate, fitting the 3â4 band with a score of 4.

The student supplies a **largely complete description of procedures**: sample collection, liquidâliquid extraction, standard preparation, paper chromatography, visualisation and data processing. Controlled, uncontrolled and safety variables are tabulated, and systematic/random error sources are discussed. However, the investigation relies on a **single, relatively simple analytical technique** without triangulation (e.g. spectrophotometry or HPLC) even though the RQ implies measuring âextentâ. Some procedural details (e.g. choice of solvent depth, justification of 0.1 mg mLâ»Â¹ standards, evaluation of extraction efficiency) are only partly justified. Hence the methodology is appropriate but not fully âcompleteâ nor demonstrably optimised, placing it at the top of the 3â4 band (score 4).

Essential layout elementsâ**title, word count (3694), page numbers, properly formatted tables/figures, and an alphabetical bibliography in a consistent style**âare present. Visuals are captioned and referenced in-text, though a few images/tables are excessively large and some links are incomplete or broken, indicating partial lapses in formatting accuracy. Despite these minor issues, the essay meets all formal presentation requirements, meriting a solid achievement within the 3â4 band; a score of 3 reflects the small but noticeable inconsistencies.

The discussion links findings to health regulations and evaluates methodological limitations, providing **some critical reflection on errors and improvements** (e.g., solvent depth, extraction losses). However, evaluation of reliability and validity is somewhat superficial, and alternative interpretations (e.g., co-elution, solvent suitability, need for quantitative confirmation) are acknowledged only briefly. The conclusion restates results but does not fully integrate them with broader chemical principles or propose detailed future work. Consequently, the discussion is **good but not consistently critical or comprehensive**, justifying a score of 4 within the 4â6 band.

The literature review draws on **a broad range of credible sources** (EFSA, JECFA, peer-reviewed journals, regulatory documents) to explain dye chemistry, health impacts and regulatory limits. These sources are integrated to justify the investigation and to interpret results. Nonetheless, some citations are repetitive, and occasional claims (e.g., prevalence of Ponceau 4R) lack direct supporting data. Overall application of sources is mostly relevant and appropriate but occasionally descriptive rather than analytical, corresponding to the upper end of the 3â4 band.

The research questionââTo what extent are the synthetic food colourants Allura Red, Ponceau 4R and Sunset Yellow FCF present in tomato sauce samples â¦ analysed through paper chromatography?ââis **explicit, focused and consistently referenced** in subsequent sections (background, methodology, results and conclusion). It is narrow enough to be investigated within an EE yet broad enough to allow discussion of findings and implications. Limitations (qualitative âpresenceâ rather than quantitative concentration) are acknowledged in the discussion. Minor ambiguity over the meaning of âto what extentâ prevents a flawless match to the top of the band, but the question remains well-formulated and strongly linked to the essayâs analysis, warranting a score of 5 in the 5â6 band.

annotations.comment: Table 1 is dense with information and disrupts flow. Consider splitting structural diagrams into separate figures and summarising functional groups in a simplified table.
The title is descriptively accurate but overly verbose. Consider condensing it to clarify the key variables and context, improving focus and immediate comprehension of the investigationâs scope.

The phrasing âto what extentâ in the research question is ambiguous. Specify whether you are investigating presence, concentration, or regulatory compliance to sharpen the scope and enable more precise methodology.
Well done including the exact word count. This fulfills the IB requirement and shows adherence to formatting conventions.
The sentence ends abruptly, likely due to an editing oversight. Complete the thought to maintain clarity and professional presentation.

The background discusses dye applications and regulations in detail, but synthesis is limited. Summarise key regulatory limits in a comparative chart to strengthen relevance and avoid repetitive narrative.
The table of contents is comprehensive but overly granular. Streamline subheadings to improve navigability and reduce redundancy, ensuring readers can quickly locate major sections.
The introduction provides broad context but lacks an explicit link back to the research question. Focus this narrative by relating consumer appeal directly to dye detection rationale.
Figure 1 is visually clear but not interpreted in the text. Explain how the complementary colour concept informs your selection of UV detection and relates to dye polarity.
The reference to Table 1 precedes the table too closely. Add a guiding sentence describing what to look for in the data before presenting the table to improve readability.
Section transitions into chemistry mechanism without tying back to how functional groups influence Rf. Critically link dye structure to expected chromatographic behaviour to deepen analysis.
The LLE procedure is described generically but lacks justification for ethanol choice and discussion of extraction efficiency; include recovery studies or solvent polarity rationale.
The stationaryâphase section conflates physical filtration and chemical adsorption. Clarify the cellulose surface chemistry and its role in dye separation mechanisms.
The regulatory limits section is comprehensive but narrative-heavy. A concise comparative table of ADIs and maximum levels would better illustrate differences between dyes.
The discussion of health implications cites many studies but lacks critical evaluation of their methodologies and relevance to streetâvendor sauces. Critically appraise source validity.
The Ponceau 4R subsection cuts off mid-sentence. Review and complete the thought to maintain professionalism and avoid reader confusion.
Sampleâcollection justification is minimal. Explain how collecting four samples ensures representativeness and discuss any potential sampling bias.
Equation for RF value is presented twice; consolidate definitions and reference the original equation to avoid redundancy and improve cohesion.
The paper chromatography description omits why water was selected as the mobile phase over standard organic eluents. Discuss solventâsample interactions and their impact on Rf values.
Mathematical notation appears inline in text rather than in separate equation blocks. Reformat key expressions using $$...$$ and number them for easy referencing in the discussion.
Full Certificates of Analysis are included inline, disrupting flow. Summarise critical purity metrics in the main text and move full CoAs to an appendix for better presentation.
Methodology tables mix narrative lists and table formats, which can confuse readers. Use concise numbered steps separate from tables to clarify each procedural stage.
The Rf equation is restated late in the methodology; refer back to its initial definition or remove this duplicate to maintain a logical flow of methods.
Sample descriptions are purely qualitative. Augment raw data with quantitative measures (e.g. pH, viscosity) to strengthen the analytical depth and relevance to chromatography results.
Standard sample Rf values are reported with ranges only. Include standard deviations or confidence intervals to provide a more rigorous uncertainty analysis.
